title: Meta Platforms Inc.
subtitle: American multinational technology conglomerate headquartered in Menlo Park, California. Owns Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads.
content_html:
Overview

Meta Platforms Inc. is an American multinational technology conglomerate that owns and operates the world's most widely used social media and messaging platforms: Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads. Founded in 2004 by Mark Zuckerberg and his Harvard roommates, the company was originally known as Facebook Inc. before rebranding to Meta Platforms on October 28, 2021 to signal a strategic pivot toward the metaverse and augmented reality.

Meta's Family of Apps reaches more than 3.35 billion daily active users across its platforms as of Q4 2024, making it the largest social media conglomerate on Earth by both users and advertising revenue. The company generated $164.5 billion in revenue in FY 2024, nearly entirely from digital advertising, and posted net income of $62.4 billion, one of the highest profit margins of any company at its scale.

Beyond social media, Meta is executing one of the largest AI infrastructure investments in the private sector, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg announcing a $65 billion AI capital expenditure plan for 2025. The company's open-weight Llama model series has positioned it as a central player in the AI race alongside OpenAI, Google, and Apple.

History

Facebook was launched on February 4, 2004, from a Harvard dorm room by Mark Zuckerberg, Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. Initially restricted to Harvard students, it expanded to other universities, then to high school students, and finally to the public in 2006. The platform reached 1 million users in its first month and never looked back.

The company's trajectory accelerated with a series of transformative acquisitions: Instagram in 2012 for $1 billion, widely regarded as the most successful technology acquisition in history; WhatsApp in 2014 for $19 billion, securing global mobile messaging dominance; and the Oculus VR acquisition in 2014 for $2 billion, which became the seed of what is now Reality Labs.

Facebook's 2012 IPO raised $16 billion at a $104 billion valuation, one of the largest tech IPOs at the time. The stock initially struggled but recovered dramatically as mobile advertising revenue surged after 2013.

The 2016 U.S. presidential election marked a turning point in public perception. Revelations about Russian disinformation campaigns on the platform and the Cambridge Analytica scandal in 2018 — in which the political data firm harvested data from 87 million Facebook users without consent — triggered congressional hearings, a $5 billion FTC settlement, and lasting reputational damage.

In October 2021, Zuckerberg rebranded the company as Meta Platforms, framing the metaverse as the next computing platform. The pivot initially drew skepticism from investors, particularly as Reality Labs losses mounted. By 2023, Meta pivoted back to AI and advertising efficiency, entering what Zuckerberg called the company's "year of efficiency," cutting 21,000 jobs and refocusing on core revenue.

Mark Zuckerberg

Mark Elliot Zuckerberg (born May 14, 1984) is the co-founder, chairman, and CEO of Meta Platforms. He has maintained majority voting control over the company through a dual-class share structure since its 2012 IPO, giving him effective control over all major corporate decisions regardless of public shareholding.

Zuckerberg's public persona shifted significantly between 2022 and 2026. After years of congressional testimony and PR crisis management, he adopted a more combative public posture, embracing Brazilian jiu-jitsu (earning a blue belt and competing in tournaments), publicly challenging Elon Musk to a cage match in 2023, and repositioning himself as a technology visionary rather than a regulated utility executive.

His net worth as of early 2026 is estimated at approximately $210 billion, making him one of the three wealthiest individuals in the world. He and his wife Priscilla Chan co-founded the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative in 2015, pledging 99% of their Meta shares to philanthropic causes focused on education, science, and justice.

Family of Apps

Facebook

Facebook remains the company's largest platform by total users with approximately 3.1 billion monthly active users as of 2024. Its user base has shifted older in Western markets, with younger demographics gravitating to Instagram and Threads. Facebook's Marketplace and Groups functions continue to generate strong engagement, and the platform remains dominant in developing markets across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Sub-Saharan Africa.

Instagram

Acquired for $1 billion in 2012, Instagram is now estimated to be worth more than $500 billion as a standalone asset — a return that makes it the most profitable acquisition in technology history. The platform has over 2 billion monthly active users, leads in influencer marketing and e-commerce integration via Instagram Shopping, and remains the dominant platform for visual brand advertising. Reels, its short-form video format launched in 2020 to compete with TikTok, now accounts for more than 50% of time spent on the platform.

WhatsApp

WhatsApp serves more than 2 billion monthly active users, primarily outside the United States, with dominant positions in India, Brazil, Germany, and most of the developing world. Meta has progressively monetized the platform through WhatsApp Business, payment integrations in India and Brazil, and click-to-WhatsApp advertising that drives traffic from Facebook and Instagram. The $19 billion 2014 acquisition price, criticized at the time, is now viewed as a bargain.

Threads

Launched in July 2023 as a direct competitor to X (formerly Twitter), Threads reached 200 million monthly active users within a year of launch, the fastest consumer app to that milestone in history. Built on the ActivityPub protocol to enable interoperability with the decentralized Fediverse, Threads has become Meta's fastest-growing platform and a key battleground in the post-Twitter social media landscape.

AI Strategy

Meta's AI strategy is defined by two parallel bets: open-weight model releases through the Llama series, and deep integration of AI into its advertising and content systems. Zuckerberg has repeatedly argued that open-sourcing AI models gives Meta a structural advantage by building developer ecosystems around its technology while making it harder for closed-model competitors to dominate on proprietary moats.

Llama

The Llama series of large language models, released openly for research and commercial use, has become the most widely deployed open-weight foundation model in the world. Llama 3 (released 2024) and subsequent versions have powered thousands of third-party applications and enterprise deployments. Meta releases Llama through its website and through Hugging Face, and the model's permissive licensing has made it the de facto standard for organizations that want to run AI on their own infrastructure.

Meta AI

Meta AI is the company's consumer AI assistant, integrated directly into Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Launched in 2023 and powered by Llama, it handles search queries, content recommendations, and conversational tasks across all four platforms simultaneously. By early 2025, Meta AI had surpassed 600 million monthly active users, placing it among the largest AI deployments globally.

Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses

Developed in partnership with EssilorLuxottica, the Ray-Ban Meta glasses embed a camera, speakers, and Meta AI into standard Ray-Ban frames. The product became Meta's first consumer hardware hit outside of VR headsets, with over 1 million units sold in the first year. A camera-equipped AI wearable that can identify objects, answer questions, and stream live video to Instagram in real time, the glasses represent Meta's most credible near-term bridge between physical and digital experiences.

Reality Labs

Reality Labs is Meta's hardware and metaverse division, responsible for the Meta Quest VR headset line, smart glasses, and longer-term augmented reality projects including a full AR headset codenamed Orion. Reality Labs has reported operating losses every year since its separation as a disclosed segment, totaling over $50 billion in cumulative losses between 2020 and 2025.

The Meta Quest 3, released in late 2023 at $499, is the best-selling standalone VR headset in the world, outselling Sony PlayStation VR2 and Apple Vision Pro. Its mixed-reality capabilities and competitive price point have kept Meta at the center of the consumer VR market, though the total addressable market remains far smaller than Zuckerberg initially projected.

Project Orion, Meta's full AR glasses prototype demonstrated in September 2024, represents the company's most ambitious hardware project. The prototype offers a wide field-of-view holographic display embedded in glasses-sized hardware, though production at scale remains technically and economically unresolved. Zuckerberg has described AR glasses as "the Holy Grail of our industry."

Advertising Business

Meta's advertising business generated $161.6 billion in FY 2024, representing over 98% of total company revenue. It is the second-largest digital advertising business in the world after Google, controlling an estimated 20% of global digital ad spend. The core product is its auction-based targeting system, which allows advertisers to reach users across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and Audience Network (third-party apps and websites) with granular demographic, interest, and behavioral targeting.

Meta's ad business suffered a significant blow in 2021 when Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework, introduced in iOS 14.5, required apps to ask users for permission before tracking them across other apps and websites. Meta estimated the impact at approximately $10 billion in lost revenue in 2022. The company has since rebuilt its ad infrastructure using on-device signal processing, AI-based conversion modeling, and expanded first-party data from Meta's own app ecosystem to reduce its dependence on cross-site tracking.

The introduction of AI-driven ad creative tools and Meta's Advantage+ automated campaign suite has driven strong advertiser adoption since 2023, with click-through rates and return-on-ad-spend metrics recovering to pre-ATT levels for many direct-response advertisers.

Controversies

Cambridge Analytica (2018)

The Cambridge Analytica scandal exposed that the political data firm had harvested personal data from approximately 87 million Facebook users without their consent, using a third-party app that exploited Facebook's then-permissive data-sharing policies. The revelation triggered congressional hearings where Zuckerberg testified for two days, a $5 billion FTC settlement in 2019 (the largest privacy fine in U.S. history at the time), and sweeping changes to Facebook's third-party data access policies.

Child Safety Verdicts (2026)

In March 2026, two separate U.S. juries handed Meta landmark defeats in child safety litigation. A New Mexico jury found Meta liable for $375 million under state consumer protection law for failing to protect minors from predatory activity on its platforms. On the same day, a Los Angeles jury found Meta liable for $3 million in a case alleging its platforms were designed to be addictive to children. Both verdicts are being appealed. For detailed coverage, see Meta's child safety verdicts, March 2026.

2025 Layoffs | Reality Labs and Social Teams

Meta launched a targeted wave of job cuts in March 2026, eliminating hundreds of positions across Reality Labs, social media teams, and recruiting as part of its ongoing pivot toward AI infrastructure. The cuts followed the company's announcement of a $65 billion AI capex plan, signaling a reallocation of headcount from consumer social to AI and hardware R&D.

Antitrust Proceedings

The FTC has pursued antitrust action against Meta since 2020, alleging that the acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp were anticompetitive and seeking a potential order to spin off both properties. The case has proceeded through multiple legal stages. A federal court dismissed the original FTC complaint in 2021, but an amended complaint was reinstated, and the case remains active heading into 2026. A ruling in the FTC's favor could be the most consequential antitrust action against a technology company since the government's case against Microsoft in the 1990s.

Financials

Key Financial Metrics (FY 2024)

    {[ ['Total Revenue', '$164.5 billion'], ['Advertising Revenue', '$161.6 billion (98.2% of total)'], ['Net Income', '$62.4 billion'], ['Net Margin', '~37.9%'], ['Free Cash Flow', '$52.1 billion'], ['AI Capex (2025 Guidance)', '$65 billion'], ['Daily Active Users (Family)', '3.35 billion'], ['Employees', '~74,000'], ].map(([label, value]) => (
  • {label} {value}
    • ))}

Meta's revenue growth re-accelerated sharply in 2023 and 2024 after the ATT-driven slowdown, driven by AI improvements to ad targeting efficiency, Reels monetization on Instagram, and strong growth in click-to-message advertising. The company's $65 billion AI capex commitment for 2025, announced in January 2025, represents the largest single-year infrastructure spending pledge by any technology company outside of cloud hyperscalers.

META shares have outperformed the NASDAQ Composite on a three-year basis, recovering from a low of approximately $88 in late 2022 to trade above $700 per share by early 2026, a gain of roughly 700% driven by the "year of efficiency" restructuring and AI-driven ad revenue growth.
